FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 3 follow-up (2019-2022), 10,048 adolescents ages 12-13 from 21 geographically diverse sites across US

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional study using multiple linear regression to estimate associations between media parenting practices and adolescent screen use

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Pediatric Research (2025) 97:403-410

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying SM/phone variables as DV: problematic social media use (SMAQ, 6-item), problematic mobile phone use (MPIQ, 8-item), problematic video game use (VGAQ, 6-item), total screen time (hours/day). Individual-trait-level variables on other side: parenting practices (media parenting—behavioral/parental variables including screen time modeling, mealtime/bedroom screen use, monitoring, limiting). SM/phone paired with individual-trait-level variables in multiple analyses.

FT_IC3.evidence: Inferential analysis: multiple linear regression models with numeric results reported throughout Table 3; e.g., parental screen time modeling associated with B: 0.82 (95% CI 0.55, 1.10) for problematic social media, B: 0.38 (95% CI 0.12, 0.64) for video games, B: 1.31 (95% CI 0.82, 1.81) for mobile phone use

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantitative results for SM ↔ individual-trait-level relationship reported: regression coefficients (β) with 95% confidence intervals and p-values for all media parenting-screen use associations (Table 3 shows 6 parenting practice categories × 4 outcomes = 24 coefficients)

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional; EC7 exclusion applies only to longitudinal designs with SM as DV in every analysis and no SM-as-IV analysis. Cross-sectional studies are exempt.
notes: Large diverse sample (48.3% female, 45.6% racial/ethnic minorities). Data overlapped COVID-19 pandemic (2019-2022) which may have influenced screen use patterns. All measures self-reported.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract explicitly states "review of findings from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study" analyzing data from over 11,000 ABCD participants; U.S. sample with typical ABCD context. Default rule applied (§2.1): no PDF but abstract clearly identifies U.S. ABCD Study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Paper is a review article. Abstract states "PURPOSE OF REVIEW: To review recent literature based on Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development Study data" with no original empirical analysis reported. Reviews, editorials, and commentaries are non-empirical per §2.2 and trigger FT-EC3.

notes: Review article of ABCD screen time/social media findings. No PDF available; excluded as non-empirical (FT-EC3).

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Period 2 follow-up (2018-2020) and Period 3 follow-up (2019-2021), 9,095 adolescents ages 10-13 years at baseline

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective cohort study using linear regression models to estimate associations between cyberbullying victimization and manic symptoms 1 year later

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Acta Paediatrica (2025) 114:1904-1909

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure is cyberbullying victimization (binary yes/no, measured as "ever experienced cyberbullying" or "past 12 months cyberbullying"). Outcome is manic symptoms (7 Up mania subscale). Cyberbullying alone without a separately-measured smartphone or social media variable does NOT qualify per §2.4: "Cyberbullying alone (with no separately-measured SM/phone variable) does NOT qualify." Paper controls for "total screen time" as a covariate but does not pair a qualifying SM/phone variable as IV or DV in any analysis.

notes: Prospective design examining cyberbullying→manic symptoms, but cyberbullying is not a qualifying SM/phone variable per v6 criteria. Failed on FT-IC2 (FT-EC2 exclusion).
